body_txt: This trope, along with Predators Are Mean , are a fairly common approach to Carnivore Confusion in fiction. Basically, all the villains are carnivores and all the herbivores, particularly if they can talk , are heroes. Like Predators Are Mean , this trope has been around for such a long time that it may be one of The Oldest Ones in the Book . Herbivores being friendly often, but not always goes hand-in-hand with predators being mean. This trope and Predators Are Mean are so strong culturally that people assume that many Real Life predators (whether omnivorous or carnivorous by nature) are mean, evil, and nasty, and all the herbivores are cute, cuddly, and friendly. This trope is not Truth in Television for the most part. Carnivores typically only attack humans out of desperation, extreme hunger or if they perceive humans as a threat, but herbivores may attack a human to claim or defend their territory, protect their offspring, drive away a potential predator, or just because they’re in a bad mood. Indeed, the most dangerous large mammals in the world are actually big herbivores; bulls, hippopotamuses and elephants are known to attack and kill humans even without being provoked. In North America, more people are killed by cattle each year than by bears , mountain lions , venomous snakes , sharks , and alligators combined . In other words, just because an animal won't eat you, doesn't mean they won't hurt you. And, while a carnivore might eat pieces of you if it kills you (or just happens to be in the area scavenging), large herbivores have an unfortunate tendency to continue to attack until your body is little more than a barely-organised mess of gore . Conversely, domesticated dogs and cats, both of whom are inclined to be friendly with humans, are inherently carnivorous - cats in particular are obligate carnivores, meaning that they exclusively eat meat. Many omnivores, such as the whale shark, are never a threat to humans. Temple Grandin 's book, Animals in Translation , explains that domesticated cattle can be a lot harder to handle compared to most felines (wild or domestic, big, small, or medium). A domestic bovine may attack a person in order to exert dominance or protect a calf, but a tiger will not do so unless their prey drive is triggered, as they are largely solitary animals that, true to nature of all cats, do not care about social hierarchy. note This is why a handler can often shoo lions off with a slipper, but a cow may even resist a whip. Additionally, carnivores need prey for food: unless a carnivore is very hungry, they will only prey on a creature when they recognize said prey as an easy meal that is unlikely to harm them (since getting injured makes it much more difficult to hunt in the future). note But note that this is somewhat different if the carnivore is social enough – if its pack will share food with it while it heals, it can afford to take more risks. Herbivores don't have the same instincts, as they can feed themselves more easily even if injured. Plus, being more aggressive comes with the added benefit of scaring off or eliminating potential threats. Also, many carnivores, particularly sharks, don't regularly interact with humans and therefore don't immediately recognize them as food. Some herbivores are portrayed as threatening and aggressive on a fairly regular basis. Boars (technically omnivorous), goats, rams , bulls , stallions , rhinoceroses , elephants , hippopotamuses , kangaroos , gorillas , ratites , and geese (technically omnivorous) are especially prone to this. When dinosaurs (not counting the before-mentioned ratites and geese) are concerned, you can expect ceratopsians , pachycephalosaurs (technically omnivorous), stegosaurs, and ankylosaurs to also be portrayed this way. On the subject of insects, expect this to be true of bees and moreso termites and mosquitoes (though they’re mostly threatening when they’re parasitic females). Another common exception, when herbivores are depicted as dangerous, is if they get involved in an Animal Stampede . Goes hand-in-hand with and opposite of Predators Are Mean . The opposite is Xenophobic Herbivore . Often associated with Carnivore Confusion . Subtrope to Good Animals, Evil Animals , Always Lawful Good , and Virtuous Vegetarianism .
